content.text: LLM SDK and Model Version Upgrade Program

LLM SDK upgrades and model version upgrades are the two most consequential change types in a GenAI application's dependency upgrade program. Both carry risks that standard dependency management tools do not surface: SDK minor versions can change how requests are constructed and responses are parsed, altering application behavior without a CVE; model version upgrades change output content, tone, and reasoning behavior in ways that functional tests cannot detect. This sub-subtopic defines a structured upgrade program for each type.

attrs.alt: LLM SDK upgrade vs model version upgrade — parallel decision paths showing evaluation stages, canary duration, and sign-off requirements

content.text: LLM SDKs (openai, anthropic, langchain, transformers) release at high frequency — often weekly. The change categories that matter for production applications are not always the ones with the largest version numbers.
messages format changed; role values changed
Requests rejected by API; silent malformed requests
tool_calls renamed to tool_use; choice index removed
Response parsing fails; tool routing breaks
Requests fail instead of retrying; or over-retry and exhaust quota
Load test with throttled mock API
New required parameter added; default changed
Requests fail or produce different output

The changelog is the primary pre-upgrade artifact. Read it thoroughly before pinning a new SDK version — not just the top-line summary but the full diff of public API surfaces. LLM SDK changelogs often bury breaking changes under 'minor improvements' headings.
The Adapter Pattern for SDK Isolation

The most common cause of painful SDK upgrades is that SDK calls are scattered across the codebase, requiring changes in dozens of files when an API surface changes. The adapter pattern isolates SDK calls behind an interface, so SDK upgrades require changes in one place.

# llm_client.py — adapter isolating the OpenAI SDK

from typing import Optional, Iterator
from dataclasses import dataclass
from openai import OpenAI as _OpenAI
from openai.types.chat import ChatCompletionMessageParam

@dataclass
class LLMResponse:
    content: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    finish_reason: str

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    id: str
    name: str
    arguments: str

class LLMClient:
    def __init__(self, api_key: str, model: str = "gpt-4o"):
        self._client = _OpenAI(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = model

    def complete(
        self,
        messages: list[ChatCompletionMessageParam],
        max_tokens: int = 1000,
        temperature: float = 0.0,
    ) -> LLMResponse:
        resp = self._client.chat.completions.create(
            model=self.model,
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            temperature=temperature,
        )
        choice = resp.choices[0]
        return LLMResponse(
            content=choice.message.content or "",
            model=resp.model,
            input_tokens=resp.usage.prompt_tokens,
            output_tokens=resp.usage.completion_tokens,
            finish_reason=choice.finish_reason,
        )

    def stream(
        self,
        messages: list[ChatCompletionMessageParam],
    ) -> Iterator[str]:
        with self._client.chat.completions.stream(
            model=self.model,
            messages=messages,
        ) as stream:
            for text in stream.text_stream:
                yield text

    def tool_call(
        self,
        messages: list[ChatCompletionMessageParam],
        tools: list[dict],
    ) -> tuple[Optional[str], list[ToolCall]]:
        resp = self._client.chat.completions.create(
            model=self.model,
            messages=messages,
            tools=tools,
        )
        choice = resp.choices[0]
        msg = choice.message

        tool_calls = []
        if msg.tool_calls:
            tool_calls = [
                ToolCall(id=tc.id, name=tc.function.name, arguments=tc.function.arguments)
                for tc in msg.tool_calls
            ]

        return msg.content, tool_calls

With this adapter, an SDK upgrade that renames a response field requires a change only in llm_client.py, not in every caller. The rest of the application uses the ToolCall and LLMResponse dataclass objects, which are unaffected by SDK internals.
between current pinned version and target version. Note every breaking change, deprecation, and behavioral difference.
(or equivalent) for all breaking changes. Do not change the adapter's public interface unless the change is additive — callers should not need to change.
— tests that verify request construction, response parsing, streaming, and tool call handling against mock SDK responses.
— the smoke test suite that calls the real API through the adapter.
— capture responses before and after. Any output change (format, length, phrasing) requires ML engineer review before promoting.

Model version upgrades are categorically different from SDK upgrades. The SDK is a client library that wraps API calls; the model is the system generating outputs. Upgrading model versions may improve reasoning quality, alter verbosity, shift the hallucination rate, or update safety behavior — and none of this produces a compilation error or a failing unit test.
Full eval + shadow eval + gradual rollout
Treat as Major regardless of model family

Shadow evaluation runs the new model version in parallel with the current model on live production traffic, without serving the new responses to users. The purpose is to accumulate real-world response pairs under actual user input distribution — not just the golden prompt set, which covers known cases but not the long tail of production inputs.

# shadow_eval.py — run new model version on production traffic shadow

import logging
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

async def shadow_complete(
    messages: list,
    current_model: str,
    shadow_model: str,
    client: AsyncOpenAI,
    log_sample_rate: float = 0.05,   # log 5% of traffic
) -> str:
    import asyncio, random

    # Always serve current model response to user
    current_task = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=current_model, messages=messages, temperature=0.7
    )

    # Shadow: call new model in parallel, don't block user response
    if random.random() < log_sample_rate:
        shadow_task = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=shadow_model, messages=messages, temperature=0.7
        )
        current_resp, shadow_resp = await asyncio.gather(
            current_task, shadow_task, return_exceptions=True
        )
        if not isinstance(shadow_resp, Exception):
            logger.info("shadow_eval", extra={
                "current_model": current_model,
                "shadow_model": shadow_model,
                "current_output": current_resp.choices[0].message.content[:200],
                "shadow_output": shadow_resp.choices[0].message.content[:200],
                "input_hash": hash(str(messages)) & 0xFFFFFF,
            })
    else:
        current_resp = await current_task

    return current_resp.choices[0].message.content

After 5 days of shadow logging, the ML engineer reviews sampled response pairs from the structured log output. The review answers: (1) are shadow responses qualitatively better, worse, or equivalent? (2) are there specific prompt categories where the new model performs poorly? (3) does the new model's verbosity or format match application expectations?
Two days before the planned upgrade window, run the full evaluation suite against the new model version in a staging environment:

: Compare output hashes and content. Categorize all changes as 'improved', 'neutral', or 'regression'. Regressions are disqualifying unless the regression count is below a defined threshold (e.g., <5% of prompts showing quality degradation).

: Run the full red-team prompt set. New model versions from the same provider family sometimes have updated safety tuning that can either improve or reduce safety filter coverage in application-specific edge cases.
: P50/P95/P99 latency and token throughput. New model versions are sometimes slower or faster than previous ones; this affects SLA attainment.

: If the new model version has different token pricing, project the monthly cost impact based on current production traffic volume. A model that produces 30% longer responses increases costs even at the same per-token rate.

Model version upgrades require explicit ML engineer sign-off on the quality delta before the upgrade window proceeds. The sign-off documents the evaluation findings and explicitly accepts or rejects the upgrade.

# Model Version Upgrade Sign-Off

## Upgrade
Current: gpt-4o-2024-11-20
Proposed: gpt-4o-2025-01-15

## Evaluation Summary
- Golden prompt comparison: 183/200 prompts identical hash, 17 changed
  - 12 changes assessed as IMPROVED (more accurate, better formatted)
  - 4 changes assessed as NEUTRAL (different phrasing, equivalent quality)
  - 1 change assessed as REGRESSION: Prompt ID gp-047 (legal clause extraction)
    - Before: correctly extracted 4 clauses
    - After: extracted 3 clauses (missed indemnification clause)
    - Severity: Medium — affects legal document use case

- Safety regression: 0 bypasses, 0 new false positives
- Latency: P99 improved from 4,200ms to 3,800ms (-9.5%)
- Cost: +3.2% due to slightly longer outputs on average

## Decision
PROCEED WITH CAUTION — legal document extraction regression noted.
Mitigation: Add test case gp-047 to integration suite. Monitor legal
document feature specifically during canary period.

Signed: [ML Engineer name]  Date: 2025-07-01
Model version canaries run longer than SDK dependency canaries because model output quality issues manifest in low-frequency input patterns that require more traffic to surface.
Error rate, safety events, CSAT proxy

The CSAT proxy during the model upgrade canary is a quality-specific signal: track the rate of support tickets tagged 'wrong answer', 'unexpected response', or 'output format issue'. A spike in these ticket types during the canary period is a quality regression signal that the golden prompt evaluation may have missed.
LLM providers deprecate old model versions on a schedule, forcing upgrades whether you are ready or not. A proactive upgrade program means deprecations are a routine event rather than an emergency.

Every model version in production should have a tracked deprecation date. When a provider announces a deprecation date, the upgrade window is scheduled so that the new model version is in full production at least 30 days before the deprecation date — leaving buffer for rollback and re-evaluation if the initial upgrade reveals problems.

# models_inventory.yaml — track all model versions in production

models:
  - id: gpt-4o-2024-11-20
    provider: openai
    env: production
    deployed_date: 2024-11-22
    deprecation_date: 2025-05-31
    upgrade_target: gpt-4o-2025-01-15
    upgrade_window_planned: 2025-04-15  # 46 days before deprecation
    evaluation_status: pending

  - id: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
    provider: anthropic
    env: production
    deployed_date: 2024-11-01
    deprecation_date: null  # not yet announced
    upgrade_target: null
    notes: "Monitor Anthropic model release cadence quarterly"
Review the models inventory monthly. When a deprecation date appears within 90 days, begin the shadow evaluation phase immediately — do not wait until 30 days before the deadline.

During a model version transition, production traffic is split between the old and new model for the canary period. This requires the application to handle both models simultaneously, which means the model version must be a runtime parameter, not a hardcoded string.

Sticky routing by user ID — routing the same user to the same model version across their session — prevents a disorienting experience where a user gets a different quality of response each time they reload. It also makes quality regression debugging easier: you can identify which users were on the canary model when a complaint was filed.

The best model version upgrades are planned, not reactive. Proactive changelog monitoring gives the engineering team advance warning before a new model version is available, time to evaluate before the forced upgrade deadline, and context to anticipate breaking changes.
Subscribe to and monitor these channels weekly:
platform.openai.com/docs/changelog, the openai-python GitHub releases page, status.openai.com for API changes
anthropic.com/news for model announcements, the anthropic-sdk-python GitHub releases, docs.anthropic.com/en/api/versioning
github.com/huggingface/transformers/releases — pin the exact version; breaking changes are common in minor versions

Assign one engineer the weekly task of reviewing these channels and filing a ticket for any upcoming change that affects production. 'I did not know about the deprecation' is a process failure, not a surprise — it means the monitoring channel was not being watched.

LLM SDK upgrades and model version upgrades share the golden prompt comparison gate but differ in evaluation depth and canary duration. SDK upgrades change the API surface between your application and the LLM provider; the adapter pattern localizes the impact of those changes. Model version upgrades change the generative behavior of the system itself; shadow evaluation, ML engineer sign-off, and 48-72 hour extended canaries are the tools that make these upgrades safe.

Provider-forced deprecations are the forcing function that makes a proactive upgrade program non-optional. A models inventory with tracked deprecation dates, evaluated at least quarterly, ensures that model version transitions are scheduled well before the deadline — giving the team time for thorough shadow evaluation and a clean canary without a 30-day countdown creating pressure shortcuts.
